Let me write the content first, then format it with the tags.

Content about Rust's ownership model:
Rust's ownership model is a fundamental concept that ensures memory safety without garbage collection. The ownership system consists of three key rules: each value has an owner, and when the owner goes out of scope, the value will be dropped. If you want to share data between different parts of your program, Rust provides borrowing mechanisms with references. Mutable variables can have only one mutable reference at any time, while immutable references allow concurrent access without data races. The compiler enforces these rules at compile time through a system called borrow checking. This approach eliminates entire classes of bugs related to null pointers and memory leaks. Rust's ownership model gives developers the performance benefits of low-level systems programming while maintaining safety guarantees typically found in higher-level languages.

I'll write a fresh version and track words carefully:

"Rust's ownership model is a fundamental concept that ensures memory safety without garbage collection by enforcing strict rules at compile time. The system consists of three key principles governing how data moves through your program. Each value has exactly one owner, meaning only one variable binds to any given piece of data at a time. When the owner goes out of scope, Rust automatically drops the value and frees memory. If you need to share data across different parts of your application, Rust provides borrowing mechanisms using references that create temporary aliases without transferring ownership. Mutable variables can have exactly one mutable reference at any moment, while immutable references allow concurrent access safely. The compiler enforces these rules through borrow checking, eliminating entire categories of bugs related to null pointers and memory leaks. This approach gives developers low-level performance benefits while maintaining safety guarantees typically found in higher-level languages."
